contract.title: Marijuana rescheduled by December 31, 2026?
contract.resolution_criteria:
This market will resolve to 'Yes' if marijuana is rescheduled down from Schedule I
to any other level (or is unscheduled completely) by the Drug Enforcement Agency of
the United States by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will
resolve to 'No'.

The primary resolution source will be official information from the U.S. government,
however a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

Note: In April 2026, FDA-approved marijuana products and state-licensed medical
cannabis were moved to Schedule III under President Trump's executive order. This
market specifically tracks whether recreational marijuana (currently Schedule I) is
rescheduled. If the DEA reschedules only specific cannabinoids or derivatives without
reclassifying marijuana as a whole, that does not qualify.

A DEA administrative hearing on full rescheduling is scheduled to begin June 29, 2026.

settlement_entities.name: Department of Health and Human Services
White House Office of National Drug Control Policy

conditions.label: DEA lowers schedule for marijuana
conditions.summary: By Dec 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, a final DEA action reclassifies marijuana (the whole plant/substance, i.e., recreational marijuana) from Schedule I to II–V. Not satisfied by rescheduling only cannabinoids/derivatives.

conditions.label: DEA unschedules marijuana
conditions.summary: By Dec 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, a final DEA action removes marijuana entirely from the Controlled Substances Act schedules (unscheduled).

contract.narrative: Regulatory pressure centers on DEA’s rescheduling rulemaking, but 2025–26 developments—state AG crackdowns, a multistate letter on federal-state enforcement, and OFAC fentanyl sanctions—are peripheral and offer no clear signal. The key federal decision remains pending; a final DEA action is still needed before the Dec 31, 2026 deadline.

timeline.title: Treasury Disrupts Sinaloa Cartel Narco-Terrorist Fentanyl Trafficking Operations
timeline.regulator: U.S. Department of the Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control — Office of Foreign Assets Control
timeline.one_line_why: OFAC sanctions targeting Sinaloa fentanyl trafficking are unrelated to DEA action on marijuana rescheduling, so no material effect on the outcome.

timeline.title: Attorney General Bonta: Joint Investigation Leads to Felony Charges Against Unlicensed Cannabis Retailer for $7.1 Million in Tax Evasion - 13.05.2026
timeline.regulator: California Department of Justice — Special Prosecutions Section
timeline.one_line_why: State tax prosecution of an unlicensed cannabis retailer is a local enforcement action and has no clear effect on the DEA's federal rescheduling decision.

timeline.title: Attorney General Alan Wilson announces indictments in State Grand Jury Operation ‘Ganjapreneur’ involving marijuana and illegal THC products being sold openly in SC businesses
timeline.regulator: South Carolina Attorney General — State Grand Jury Division
timeline.one_line_why: South Carolina Attorney General — State Grand Jury Division — South Carolina Securities Commissioner: tangential to resolution, no directional effect.

timeline.title: 2026-01-28 - Attorney General Bonta Joins Multistate Coalition Condemning U.S. DOJ Threats Against Minnesota
timeline.regulator: California Department of Justice Office of the Attorney General
timeline.one_line_why: Multistate AGs condemn DOJ threats; this concerns federal-state enforcement politics but gives no direct signal on DEA's rescheduling decision.

timeline.title: AG Campbell Secures Agreement With Trump Administration Preventing Further Delays In Medical And Public Health Research
timeline.one_line_why: Office of the Attorney General — Massachusetts Office of the Attorney General Consumer Advocacy & Response Division: tangential to resolution, no directional effect.

primary_drivers: Duplicate press releases announcing felony charges from a joint investigation
AG urging federal crackdown on xylazine ('tranq') amid an abuse surge
Multistate coalition actions and a federal agreement drawing public attention
heat_panel.explainer: Critical heat is driven by recent high‑visibility AG activity — repeated felony‑charge announcements from a joint probe, renewed calls to crack down on xylazine ('tranq'), and multistate legal actions.
